data.title: RF Industries: Better Margins, Better Demand, Better Stock
data.description:
I thought RF Industries (RFIL) was worth a second look as I researched it, because this stock seems uninteresting at first. For starters, it’s a small company that designs and makes interconnected products. RF Industries sells products that enable communication and industrial systems to operate in the background, like cable assemblies, connectors, custom wiring, small cell systems, and cooling products.

It mostly means making the hardware that keeps networks and other infrastructure running. It may not sound very interesting, but companies that do this can still be good investments because these things are foundational. Nobody can live without having their smartphones nearby, and as the world moves towards globalization, it's companies like these that don’t really have the spotlight, and I think that’s where value truly lies.

Fiscal 2025 was a much better year for the company. Revenue grew by 24.2% to reach $80.6 million, gross margin rose by 29.1% to 33.2%, and the operating income recorded a gain after being negative last year.

I believe that a Buy rating for RFIL stock can be justified by the fact that it has been improving, and more to the point, I continue to believe that the stock is undervalued. My opinion is anchored on three factors: that margins are improving, there seems to be expansion in the demand over products and end markets, and the current valuation does not completely reflect the business recovery to steady profitability.

What the Business Does

RF Industries sells hardware that helps networks and other systems connect and operate, which includes cable assemblies, RF connectors, wiring harnesses, fiber-related products, small-cell systems, and cooling products. I believe that the product mix of RF Industries’ products is important, as it is not limited to a single level of the connectivity stack. As indicated in the 10-K, the company does not just fit into a single, small category; it markets RF connectors, adapters, coaxial and data cables, wire harnesses, fiber optic products, custom cabling, cooling systems, and integrated small-cell enclosures.

That breadth plays an operationally significant role as it provides the company with various opportunities to engage in customer deployments rather than employing only one product family, making them more competitive than a simple catalog-parts supplier, even though some of the categories remain obviously competitive.

I believe that the connector and cable sides remain foundational, although it is most likely the least differentiated aspect of the portfolio. According to the filing, RF Industries has over 1,500 connector, adapter, tool, and test-kit types available, as well as over 100,000 cable-assembly combinations, both standard and custom.

I am reading between the lines that such a scale and size make the company relevant in a variety of uses, particularly since such products can serve thousands of devices and applications. I do not view this as the strongest moat, because the company also says the RF Connector division carries thousands of products, most of which are available in standard sizes and configurations and are also offered by competitors.

I found the highest-value integrated products of the company, particularly passive RF products, thermal control systems, and small-cell enclosures, to be the most interesting area in the product range. The 10-K reports that Microlab produces passive RF and microwave components such as dividers, directional couplers, and filters used in DAS, wireless base stations, and small-cell networks, and Schrofftech produces intelligent thermal cooling systems and pole-ready small-cell shrouds and enclosures. I believe that the categories are more strategically significant since they are addressing signal management and installation and operating environment issues instead of simply addressing a simple connector request, which to me is a good indicator that RF Industries is attempting to shift towards products that are in closer proximity to the system-level problem of the customer, which precisely is the direction I would like to see.

To me, that is one sign that RF Industries is trying to move closer to the customer’s broader system-level problem. That interpretation also lines up with management’s more recent language on the Q4 2025 earnings call, where executives described the business as evolving from a component supplier toward a more solutions-oriented model.

Why Fiscal 2025 Mattered

RFIL’s revenue rose to $80.36 million, which was up 24.2% from the prior year; gross profit increased to $26.14 million, and gross margin rose to 33.2% from 29.1%. Operating income also improved to $1.8 million from an operating loss of $2.8 million in fiscal 2024, indicating that in 2025 the company had better scale, a better mix, and better execution.

During the Q4 2025 call, CFO Peter Yin indicated that gross margin increased to 37% in the fourth quarter, compared to 31%, and that the growth was brought about by increasing sales and a more friendly product mix.

The management also mentioned the high contribution of integrated systems like small cell and DAC thermal cooling, and full-year 2025 results were propelled by its shipments of the custom cabling business to one of the major aerospace customers.

The increase of gross margin by 250 basis points over the year in Q1 2026 to 32.3% was still reported to be price realization, efficiency in its operations, and control of costs, which were said by management. And that to me is the most obvious indication that RF Industries is not merely enjoying the benefits of volume but of selling more of the types of products and solutions with better economics.

Retention of customers must also be linked with something more tangible as opposed to my opinion. During the Q4 2025 call, management also indicated that it was seeking to build deeper relationships with current customers, deploy higher-value proprietary offerings as the tip of the spear, and then cross-sell more products once it was collaboratively working with the technical and market teams of the customer.

Customers are also approaching RF Industries sooner in their design cycles, which is important, the management says, since once a supplier is brought on board in the earlier stages of specification and design, the relationship is more likely to be long-lasting and the portion of wallet can increase over time.

I would not refer to that as evidence of retention in the strict numeric sense, but I believe it is reasonable evidence that the management is making an intentional effort to make customer relationships more valuable and sticky.

If revenue rose but profits stayed weak, I would’ve asked more questions, but since profits and margins improved along with revenue, no questions asked there. Alongside rising revenues and improving margins, operating cash flow totaled $4.6 million in 2025, suggesting the earnings improvement because it’s the highest it has been in 5 years.

The Latest Quarter Looks Better

Having said that, the large-project comment remains relevant since it demonstrates how RF Industries can be when it comes to the timing of projects. The management did not report that previous-year project as a repeat source of demand; instead, CEO Rob Dawson treated it as a "welcome anomaly" and so it should have been considered as a gain to the comparison time period and not a sustenance into future operating activity.

The table numbers can assist in demonstrating the importance of such a difference. It achieved $18.97 million against $19.20 million revenue in the year before, and hence the sales dropped by only 1.2% annually, which resulted in a higher profitability. EBITDA rose to $1.06 million from $0.87 million, up 22.2%, while EBITDA margin increased to 5.58% from 4.52%, a gain of 107 basis points. EBIT also improved to $0.18 million from $0.06 million, up 216.1%, and EBIT margin rose to 0.93% from 0.29%. This to me means that that current quarter was not being supported with the same type of one-off project benefit, but instead it was reporting greater underlying profitability on about similar revenue.

What is more important to future operating performance is whether the company can substitute that type of one-off benefit with more widespread and more repeatable demand.

The recent commentary by management is headed in that direction because in the Q1 2026 call, Dawson noted that backlog increased in various areas, such as small cell, DAC thermal cooling, custom cabling to aerospace, and custom cabling to industrial customers, and CFO Peter Yin reminded investors once again that backlog is just a snapshot in time and can fluctuate based on the timing and completion of orders.

This does not eliminate volatility but does indicate that the future operating performance might be less dependent on any specific large project that seems unusual as compared to the previous-year quarter.

Backlog and Demand

Backlog is improved, and the management was attentive not to oversell it. During the Q1 2026 call, CFO Peter Yin reported that the backlog at Q4 2025, based on $17.9 million in bookings, was $14.4 million and indicated an increase to $18.6 million by the time of the call. He also commented that backlog is merely a "snapshot in time" and can shift around as orders are received and processed and thus not to be used as an indicator of future revenue but merely as a general health indicator.

Management said that the rise was not due to a single order and their backlog growth was an indication of a healthy mix in small cell, DAC thermal cooling, custom cabling to aerospace, and custom cabling to industrial customers, said CEO Rob Dawson. That is important because it implies that the demand is becoming widespread throughout the business and not a single project with an exceptionally high cost.

Meanwhile, management failed to offer backlog as an indication that growth will be smooth sailing. CFO Peter Yin repeated that backlog can swing significantly between periods, and Dawson indicated that the bigger backlog helps management anticipate that revenue would be better in Q2 than in Q1 and that it would be growing thereafter. I have learned that the trend of the backlog is promising, yet it must be translated into shipments before it can be considered as solid evidence of the continued growth.

Margins and Balance Sheet

The margins are the main reason I like this stock. It's great that sales are going up, but better margins are what will really change how the market sees a company like this. The gross margin went up from 29.1% to 33.2% in fiscal 2025. In the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, management talked about a gross margin of about 37% and an adjusted EBITDA margin of about 11.5%, which was higher than its long-term goal of 10% for adjusted EBITDA margin. If RF Industries can keep its margins in the low 30% range and keep growing its higher-value product categories, then profits can grow faster than sales. That's when a stock usually starts to change its rating. Investors usually don't pay much for a small company with uneven margins, but they will pay more for a business that is showing it can grow and make money.

I don't think the balance sheet is a big deal either. The company had $5.1 million in cash and about $7.1 million drawn on its revolver at the end of the first quarter of fiscal 2026. That means the total amount of debt was only about $2.0 million. Management also said that net debt was lower than it had been a year ago.

That level of debt seems reasonable to me for a small business that just started making money and improving its cash flow. It's not perfect, but it's not the kind of stress on the balance sheet that would make me stay away from the stock. RF Industries still needs to keep working, but it doesn't look like it's in a financial bind. That gives management some time to keep putting money into the best parts of the business and gives investors a little more peace of mind if growth doesn't happen as quickly as they thought it would.

Valuation

The first important assumption is growth, so I went with 13% revenue growth for FY2026 and 14% for FY2027. A lot of changes can steer these assumptions, like backlog improving, bookings being solid, and a broader demand instead of RFIL leaning on one big project.

In the DCF below, I also used a 34.5% gross margin for FY2026 because it's the right middle ground; it's above the 33.2% the company delivered for FY2025 but still below the 37% management talked about in Q4. In the end, this doesn't automatically mean something big will happen down the line; it's just RFIL might continue to keep its current gross margins.

This model also uses an 8% EBITDA margin in FY2026 and 10.5% in fiscal 2027 because RFIL is still seen as a small, uneven hardware business, but if it can move towards management’s long-term goal of 10%+ adjusted EBITDA margin, then I don't think the stock will stay valued as is.

Put two and two together, and these assumptions produce an EV of about $143.05m and an equity value of about $141.04m, or roughly $13.05 per share, and compare that with today's price at $10.44; the model shows a 25% upside.

To me, that upside works because it comes from a pretty simple chain of logic, and I'm not saying that RF Industries is dramatically mispriced, but if we're using FY2025 as a basis and considering the macro trends where markets have been unfavorable and the fact that they're getting broader demand, then to me, the stock still looks undervalued.

Risks

Among the criticisms that would be better discussed in more direct terms is the fact that RF Industries has historically developed a large portion of its platform by acquisition, as opposed to organic growth by itself.

The present business comprises Cables Unlimited, Rel-Tech, Schrofftech, C Enterprises, and Microlab, all of which have been acquired over the years, with Microlab in 2022 being the most recent major acquisition. Therefore, I do not believe that it would be just to portray RF Industries as an organic compounder. Meanwhile, the recent recovery does not seem to be acquisition-based in the short term, since there has not been a new big deal behind the improvement of fiscal 2025. Rather, the management has been reporting the recovery as being in terms of improved product mix, enhanced execution, expanded end-market exposure, and enhanced contribution by small cell, DAC thermal cooling, RF passive solutions, and custom cabling. I believe that the present recovery is more of an operational than an acquisitive one, but the business is small and project-based to the extent that organic growth will probably continue to be uneven quarter to quarter.

Another risk is that the company is highly reliant on wireless and broadband communications providers and cautions that any cyclical decline in the communications industry would have a significant adverse impact on demand, revenue, and profitability.

The management stated on the Q4 2025 call that telecom and wireless have reduced from about 70% of sales in previous years to about 50% today, which is significant progress, but a significant portion of the business is still linked to carrier, broadband, and other infrastructure budgets. Should those markets falter once again, particularly in a small firm where project-based income is the rule, things could go downhill fast.

The other risk is that the increase in profitability can be more volume sensitive than the market anticipates. The management has repeatedly indicated that increased sales, improved mix, and operating leverage are collaborating to push up margins.

That is positive on the uphill side, but it also implies that the model can be used in reverse in case projects are postponed, or telecom or infrastructure expenditure decelerates, or higher-value product lines like DAC, small cell, and custom cabling fail to scale. RF Industries might still be profitable in that case, but the road to a higher multiple would be a lot less obvious.

If revenue growth doesn't come back in the second half of 2026, the stock may already be closer to fair value than I would like. Still, when I look at the good things and the bad things, I tend to be on the bullish side. RFIL looks stronger, the margins look better, the demand trends look good, and the valuation still leaves room for growth. For me, that's enough to give it a Buy rating.

Conclusion

Overall, I still think RF Industries is a buy because the company is starting to show real improvement and not just small, short-term hype. The business made more money, margins got better, demand looked more spread out, and the company does not seem to be under major financial pressure. What stands out to me is that the stock still looks fairly cheap compared to the progress the company has already made. So even if it is not a perfect business, I think there is enough going right here to believe the upside still outweighs the risks.

data.title: VCR: Consumer Discretionary Sector Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly column provides a top-down analysis of the consumer discretionary sector, based on value, quality and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR) and Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The value score has deteriorated across the sector, mostly due to price action.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

The consumer services and durables/apparel sub-sectors are very close to their 11-year averages in valuation, and additionally the former boasts an excellent quality score. The retail subsector is overvalued by about 20%, while the auto and components industry has the lowest value and quality scores.

Focus on VCR

Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF was launched on 01/26/2004 and tracks the MSCI US IMI Consumer Discretionary 25/50 Index. The same strategy is available as a mutual fund (VCDAX). VCR has a portfolio of 286 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.09%, while XLY has 48 holdings and charges 0.08%. Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (FDIS) tracks almost the same index for 0.08% fees.

VCR has very high company-specific risk, with more than 40% of asset value in Amazon and Tesla, and 61% in the top 10 holdings, listed below.

In summary, VCR provides cap-weighted exposure in the consumer discretionary sector with a broader portfolio than XLY, but the two funds are equivalents for long-term investors. Traders will prefer XLY for its higher trading volumes, and investors concerned by both fund's exposure in Amazon and Tesla may choose Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Discretionary ETF (RSPD).

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a retail company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0461 (or price/earnings below 21.69) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

data.title: Tenaz Energy: An Important Call Option On European Natural Gas Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Now that the oil and European gas prices are normalizing after the Iran-related price run, I want to have another look at Tenaz Energy (TNZ:CA) (ATUUF). I have been keeping track of this company for quite a while now, but the anticipated production growth in 2026 and 2027 may catch the market by surprise (although clearly signaled by the company). I have always maintained a long-term European natural gas price of 25-35 EUR per MWh, and Tenaz Energy should benefit from that price range.

A Look at the Expectations for 2026

For the current financial year, the company anticipates to produce between 19,500 and 22,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day. I would like to emphasize that this is just the average, as the company anticipates to exit this year with an attributable production that could be as high as 27,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day.

The production growth comes on the back of acquisitions that were completed in 2025, while a total of 3.1 to 3.6 net new wells will be drilled in the Netherlands (in a combination of wells drilled on the operated assets as well as on the non-operated assets). Additionally, the company will drill three wells in Canada, representing a net attributable well count of 2.6.

And while total production is expressed in barrels of oil equivalent, this actually is a natural gas company, as 93% of the anticipated production mix for this year will consist of natural gas. And the vast majority of this is European natural gas, subject to the European TTF natural gas price (which is a multiple of the North American natural gas price, with current prices north of US$10/mmbtu).

A key advantage of operating offshore in the Netherlands is the fact that there is no royalty payable, and although the statutory tax rate of 46% is high, it represents approximately 1/4 of the pre-tax funds from operations. This makes the total tax pressure quite manageable and not as prohibitive as, for instance, UK North Sea operations.

Unfortunately (although it is good practice), the company has hedged the majority of its anticipated natural gas production in Europe at an average in the low €30 range per MWh, while approximately 30% of the 2027 output has been hedged as well. But as you can see below, there's plenty of room to add more hedges for both 2027 as well as starting to roll out hedges for 2028 now that the TTF price levels remain at solid levels.

One of the key elements I will be looking forward to when the company publishes its results for the first quarter of the current financial year is to see whether or not it took advantage of the very strong TTF natural gas prices to lock in additional hedges. Just to give you an example, the TTF price for delivery in Q3 2027 was trading above 40 EUR per MWh in the past few weeks, so this would have been a good opportunity to increase the ceiling price in the hedge book.

I anticipate the total production in 2027 to come in at approximately 30,000 to 32,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day (with natural gas still representing almost the entire oil equivalent output). Using a TTF natural gas price of C$13/mcf and C$81/barrel for oil, Tenaz expects a total FFO of approximately C$500M, and in combination with a lower capex requirement of around C$175-200M, this could pave the way to C$300M in net free cash flow.

That would mean that the total amount of net debt (which stood at almost C$350M as of the end of last year) should evaporate fast, and with a little bit of luck, the company will be debt-free by the end of next year (subject to the contingent consideration payments as part of the recent acquisitions). This could pave the way to pursue more M&A (keep in mind the management team is the team that built Vermilion Energy (VET) (VET:CA) from the ground up) with Tenaz pursuing a roll-up strategy.

The Reserves: Okay, but Could Be Higher

One key element that could have some room for improvement is the total amount of reserves on the assets. While the company touts a reserve life index of 16 years based on the 2P reserves (shown below), this number is based on the 2025 production numbers. Once the consolidated output increases to in excess of 30,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, this reserve life index will drop to a [high] single-digit number.

This also means the current market cap and enterprise value of Tenaz exceed the after-tax PV10 value of the cash flows (which stood at C$1.6B as of the end of last year). After deducting the almost C$350M in net debt, the PV10 value comes in at C$1.25B, or around C$40 per share (rounded). The PV10 calculation is based on a TTF natural gas price of just over US$10/mmbtu in the next three years. So a step-up in the TTF natural gas price will bring cash flows forward and reduce the impact of the cumulative discount rate.

While not a deterrent at this moment, it would be good to see Tenaz working on converting some of its resources into a 2P reserve category to ensure the reserve life index remains above 10 years, even at the increased production rate.

Investment Thesis

Tenaz’s management has made it quite clear that M&A is a very realistic possibility (it ‘has a very good transaction pipeline in place’), and I am looking forward to see what kind of assets Tenaz is able to acquire and at what cost.

While I think this year’s net free cash flow will remain below C$100M due to a capex push and a rather moderate production increase, expectations for 2027 are much higher. I wouldn't be surprised to see a total net free cash flow of approximately C$300M based on a 30-35 EUR/MWh TTF natural gas price in 2027, which would represent C$9/share based on the current share count.

I currently have no position in the company, but I will be looking to establish an initial long position in the next few weeks, taking advantage of the current weakness in its share price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alamar Biosciences: Looking For An Early Detection
data.description:
Shares of Alamar Biosciences (ALMR) have seen a very successful public offering. Shares have risen 30% on the first day of trading, with investors being very upbeat on its precision proteomics technology for early disease detection. I like the mission of the business a lot, its potential as well, as the same applies for the sales momentum demonstrated upon.

While some operating deleverage has been seen in the first quarter, overall momentum is strong, as I do not fear the losses given these strong cash balances. The strong sales momentum is very welcome, and while expectations have become a bit more demanding, they are not outrageous given the prospects here.

For now I am keeping a very close eye on the future reports of the business, looking to learn more about the topline growth trends, and the impact on the bottom line, before considering a position here.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Precision Proteomics For Early Disease Detection

Alamar has a mission to power precision to enable the earliest detection of disease. The company was founded by Yuling Luo, a serial founder and entrepreneur in the life science business. In a family event, he recognizes that the most effective treatment for patients suffering from cancer and other diseases is that of detection, with proteins playing an instrumental role in that.

Based on this insight, the business was founded in 2018. With detection being key in the early part of the diseases, this is just the time when the biological signals are the weakest. That results in many false positives, but the company finds a solid balance in this, creating real insights. Currently such solutions are only used in research settings, not authorized for clinical usage yet, but of course being applied in such settings is among the goals of the business here.

The company positions itself as conservative, claiming a culture and playbook of under-promising and over-delivering, committed to building a principled culture and building the company in stages.

The company has precisely built its NULISA technology to address limitations of existing proteomics tools by detecting protein biomarkers at very low concentrations in non-invasive fluids. The ARGO High Throughput (HT) system was launched in January 2024, and by now has been used by more than 300 customers across 25 countries. Besides this system, the company provides consumables and services as well.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Alamar aimed to sell 9.4 million shares in a preliminary price range between $15 and $17 per share, with pricing set at the high end of the range at $17 per share. In total, some 11.2 million shares were sold by the company, which ended up fetching $191 million in gross proceeds.

With some 66.5 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $1.13 billion equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net cash position of just over a quarter of a billion, for an operating asset valuation just below $900 million here.

The company reported revenues just north of $25 million in 2024, these revenues being accompanied by a nearly $50 million operating loss. Real gains have been seen in 2025, with revenue essentially tripling to $74 million, while operating losses narrowed to $31 million.

The company has not reported revenues for each quarter, yet the preliminary numbers for the first quarter of 2025 reveal that the first quarter revenues for 2025 came in at $13 million. More important is that first-quarter revenues for 2026 are seen around $25.7 million, trending around a hundred million each year. So far so good, as the business is trading around 9 times sales here, these revenues nearly doubling on an annual basis.

Operating losses are seen ticking up to a midpoint of $12.6 million, trending around $50 million each year here, suggesting some deleveraging from the 2025 results, albeit that the net cash holdings are substantial enough to make investors not worry about this.

Shares have risen by nearly 30% on the first day of trading, now exchanging hands at $22 per share. This has pushed up the operating asset valuation to more than $1.2 billion, pushing up annualized sales multiples to around 12 times sales here, this now still being a loss-making business.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks in the offering are plentiful, including demanding valuations, losses being reported, reliance on a single product, reliance on key staff, changes in the regulatory field, changes in competitive technologies, etc.

Peers include smaller, focused names like Quanterix (QTRX), which has been struggling in a huge manner. Olink, owned by Thermo Fisher (TMO), is part of a much larger and diversified peer.

This makes it very difficult to make realistic comparisons here, as the business is a bit unique in its form. I like the business a great deal, its mission, and its sales momentum. While I am not chasing the shares right now, I am perfectly happy to keep a very close eye on the trends going forward.

data.title: First Reliance Bancshares: This Micro Bank Shouldn't Be Overlooked
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $109.7 million, First Reliance Bancshares (FSRL) is one of the smaller banks that I have looked at. But in recent years, management has succeeded in growing the revenue and profits of the business. Asset quality for the institution looks positive, and credit quality metrics check out. There has been some volatility when it comes to the balance sheet, but nothing absurd. And when you consider how cheap the stock is on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar terms, I would argue that rating it a soft ‘buy’ makes sense here.

Of course, the picture could change as new data comes in. Soon, we will have just that. Management is expected to announce financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year on April 24th. Leading up to that date, the good news is that analysts are optimistic about the company, with revenue and profits forecasted to rise year over year. So long as we don't see any major unexpected developments that negatively impact the outlook for the company, I believe that my rating here will hold.

It’s worth banking on First Reliance Bancshares

For the uninitiated, First Reliance Bancshares is a rather interesting bank, having been incorporated back in 1999. The firm has historically operated in markets throughout South Carolina and North Carolina. And through the branches that it has in operation, it offers up traditional banking services. Given its size, it really doesn't do much more beyond that. However, management has been making a concerted effort to grow the company in recent years.

In the chart above, you can see the balance sheet for the company. From 2023 through 2025, deposits increased from $858.6 million to $948.1 million. As deposits increased, loans grew also. They expanded from $697.3 million to $771.1 million. Primarily, the company focuses on commercial real estate. About $466.3 million, or 59.8%, of its loan portfolio is tied up in commercial real estate. We also have another $230.8 million, or 29.5%, invested in consumer real estate. All other categories are quite small by comparison, with commercial and industrial loans, not to mention consumer and other miscellaneous loans, making up just a small piece of the overall pie.

Usually when you see deposits increase, you see other aspects of the balance sheet grow as well. And sure enough, that is what has transpired. Over the last three years, securities expanded from $172.5 million to $197.8 million. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents increased from $21.9 million to $32.1 million. There was, according to the data available, a corresponding increase in debt. But it was relatively small, rising from $31 million to $39.8 million.

One thing that I can definitely appreciate about First Reliance Bancshares is the fact that, as time has gone on, the overall book value and tangible book value for the business have increased. Continued success when it comes to profitability has allowed the firm to see a rise in book value per share from $8.77 to $11.88. Printable book value rose in tandem, climbing from $8.68 per share to $11.79 per share. At the end of the day, this is evidence of a quality management team creating real value for shareholders.

Naturally, a larger balance sheet opens the door to greater revenue and profitability. And sure enough, that is precisely what we have seen. Over the last three years, net interest income jumped from $28.6 million to $36 million. Even more impressive was the rise in non-interest income from $6.9 million to $12.7 million. I think it is important to point out here that the firm has continued to benefit from an expansion and its net interest margin. Even though there has been just a small drop in the cost of deposits as of late, with that figure dropping from 2.68% in 2024 to 2.50% in 2025, the high-interest rate environment allowed the institution to capture some rather hefty returns. The yield on loans has increased from 5.12% to 5.80% over the last three years. Securities, meanwhile, grew from 3.81% to 4.68%. As a result of all of this, net interest margin rose from 3.19% to 3.61% over the course of just three years.

As revenue increases, profitability rises as well. The firm has seen its net profit more than double from $4.6 million to $10.9 million over the last three years. This surge in profitability has had a big impact on the valuation of the company. In the chart above, you can see that the company is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple right now of 10.1. That's just a hair above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. In that chart, you can also see how the institution stacks up against five other similar firms. Only one of them is cheaper than it is on this basis. The chart below shows a similar ranking when it comes to both the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. On a price-to-book basis, only one of these institutions ended up being cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, when it comes to the price of the tangible book approach, none of them were cheaper.

Asset quality for the company has increased over the last few years as well. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets from 2023 through 2025, as well as how the institution stacks up against the same five banks that I compared it to already. With three of the five banks coming in lower than what it boasts, it's not exactly a standout prospect in this regard. But the fact of the matter is that the return on assets reading of 1% is exactly the minimum that I typically like to see in this space. In the subsequent chart below, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. The rise from 7% to 12.90% over the last three years has been impressive. What's more, the firm is superior to any of the five institutions in that chart, plus the 12.90% reading that we get for 2025 puts the institution above the 10% minimum that I typically like to see.

Lastly, we should touch briefly on credit quality. In general, I like to see that no more than 0.50% of a bank’s assets are classified as non-performing. In the chart below, you can see that First Reliance Bancshares has experienced a rise in this metric in recent years. But it's not even half of the way to my maximum threshold. That is fantastic to see. This signifies to me that while the overall trend is a slight negative, the absolute reading for the company is low enough to justify optimism.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year around the end of this month. As we approach that date, it's worth noting that analysts expect revenue to rise from $11.2 million last year to $12.3 million this year. They also expect earnings per share to jump from $0.19 to $0.32, taking net profits up from $1.6 million to $2.6 million. In all likelihood, the firm will deliver something similar to this, considering how well it has performed over the last couple of years now. But investors should obviously keep a close eye on the data that does come out.

Takeaway

The way I see it, First Reliance Bancshares is not some home run prospect. However, it's far from a bad one. The institution has done a good job of improving financial performance in recent years. Long term, it's likely that continued growth will occur. When you consider how cheap the company is, on top of certain asset quality and credit quality metrics, I see no reason not to be bullish enough to rate it a soft ‘buy.’

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Thank You, Seniors. Healthcare Spending Is Fueling Economic And Job Growth - THQ
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

A Structural Tailwind Too Big to Ignore

Some of the most important data points that help us gauge the health of the economy are inflation prints, job reports, and consumer spending. Increasingly, all three are pointing in the same direction and toward the same critical sector.

The United States is the largest consumer market in the world, but in 2025, healthcare was the largest contributor to consumer spending, possibly indicating an inflection point in the silver tsunami thesis. The U.S. is also the largest economy in the world, and the nation also has the highest healthcare spending globally, consistently at 17–18% of its GDP.

The labor market also points to healthcare as a driver of the U.S. economy. With the weight of the tariffs, higher inflationary pressures, and AI disruption fears, we are seeing slowness in hiring across many industries. Still, healthcare continues to add jobs at a steady pace. In March 2026, the health care sector added 76,000 jobs, representing the anchor for U.S. employment growth and serving as a source of stability.

Healthcare has maintained a lead over baseline inflation for more than two decades, and there are compelling reasons that this trend will continue. The most powerful driver of all is demographics. By 2030, Americans over the age of 65 are expected to outnumber those under 18 for the first time in history. Healthcare is the largest, fastest-growing, and most unpredictable expense for older people, creating a demand tailwind that is predictable and persistent.

If we put these together, these trends highlight a sector that is less tied to economic swings and more driven by structural forces. Healthcare is helping define the future of the U.S. economy, and legislation cannot stop this.

Not an Income-Focused Sector

The healthcare sector is not one that is generous with dividends. In fact, healthcare is not going to be in the top ten sectors that are known for their dividend consistency, payouts, or track record. But it is an essential sector, providing necessary goods and services that are often exclusive, with patent protections that stretch over decades. Take a look at Humira, from AbbVie (ABBV), one of the best-selling drugs in global history. Humira was first approved in 2002, and biosimilars did not arrive until 21 years later, in 2023. Even after biosimilars appeared, the market had been dominated for so long that switching was slow and often dependent on insurance policies. Now you have Keytruda from Merck (MRK), which is one of the fastest-growing oncology drugs. Keytruda was first approved in 2014 and enjoys patient protection until 2028-30.

To effectively invest in healthcare, you need to be an expert in the industry, which isn’t easy. Medical and healthcare professionals spend decades specializing in highly specific areas, often narrowing their expertise to small sub-domains. Because of this specialization, no single professional can be expected to intelligently discuss every top-selling drug on the market.

If I need to upgrade the breaker box in my house, I am not going to do it myself. Even though I have a degree in Electrical Engineering, and understand how circuits and fuse boxes work, this requires a certain set of skills and experience that I do not have. It is ok to rely on professionals to do the work for you. At High Dividend Opportunities, we don't shy away from seeking the services of experts to fulfill our investment objectives.

Capturing High Yields From a Resilient Sector

abrdn Healthcare Opportunities Fund (THQ) is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that is actively managed, with healthcare equipment, biotechnology, pharmaceuticals, and life sciences companies as its top holdings. Not surprisingly, THQ’s top holdings are the aforementioned ABBV, MCK, diabetes care leader Abbott Laboratories (ABT), GLP-1 leader Eli Lilly (LLY), robotic surgery leader Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), animal health leader Zoetis, etc. THQ’s top 10 positions represent 37% of the fund’s invested assets. Source

The average yield from these positions is probably less than 2%. As we discussed, the healthcare sector is not one that is known for its generous payouts. The fund managers, formerly Tekla Capital Management, now abrdn, focus on realizing gains from asset sales and NAV gains to support regular monthly distributions. Looking at the total returns over the past decade, THQ has outperformed the healthcare equipment, pharma, and biotech benchmark indices.

The healthcare sector saw steep drawdowns over this timeframe, but THQ has maintained its monthly payouts, providing much-needed cash to reinvest at distressed prices. In addition, THQ has done a terrific job with NAV maintenance since its inception.

THQ distributes $0.18/month, which calculates to an 11.9% annualized yield. The bulk of the payout in recent years has been long-term capital gains, or return of capital, making it advantageous from a tax standpoint.

THQ isn’t a compromise simply because it prioritizes income. The fund has outperformed sector benchmarks over the long term and has maintained its book value over time. This makes it a well-managed fund, suitable for our long-term income needs.

Conclusion

While the definition of “essential” has expanded with our desire for convenience and comfort, a few industries still form the baseline for human survival. Healthcare is one of them, and it will continue to be in the darkest of times.

It isn't easy to invest in this sector, as products, services, and innovation always sound promising and groundbreaking. And then there is the exclusivity period, peak revenue, TAM estimation, and competition from biosimilars. It takes a qualified professional in the industry to crunch the complex concepts and make the right investment moves to generate attractive returns. With THQ, you get a team of experts that actively manages a portfolio of healthcare names to deliver monthly income into your account.

data.title: Zepp Health: A Riskier Bet As Gross Margins Compress (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 has skyrocketed to fresh all-time highs, and many value-oriented investors like myself are wondering the same thing: are there still good value stocks to buy? In my view, now is an excellent time to be an active investor, pruning positions from our portfolios that no longer meet the bill in order to redeploy capital into unloved sectors, particularly in the software space, where many bargains abound.

Zepp Health (ZEPP), a Chinese smartwatch company, is a name I'm gradually souring on. Though the stock has become more of a value play after a ~40% slide this year, I'm becoming more nervous around the company's inability to turn a profit. In a more risk-averse market environment where more investors are demanding to see tech companies' profits, I'm no longer certain there's a light at the end of Zepp Health's pain.

I last wrote a buy article on Zepp in November, when the stock was trading at $24 per share. Since then, Zepp has nosedived, and though the stock has been a blight in my portfolio, I'm losing the patience to see through a turnaround in this name. Though I still consider Zepp's growth to be best-in-class, I'm dropping my rating on the stock to neutral.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for this name. On the bright side for this company:

  • Zepp's Amazfit lineup is achieving substantial growth for the company. Revenue is growing north of >40% y/y, as the company is buoyed by successful recent product launches. Compare this to mid-teens growth for the fitness division at Garmin (GRMN), one of its major rivals, and ~flat performance for the wearables, home, and accessories segment at Apple (AAPL), which doesn't provide a specific breakout for the Apple Watch.
  • Striking high-end partnerships that are legitimizing its product in the fitness community. The company just signed a three-year deal with HYROX, an athletic race that is gaining substantial fans and attention globally. This partnership not only elevates Amazfit's brand in the fitness community, but could also see the development of more fitness technology beyond watches, such as rings and tracking straps.

At the same time, however, I am concerned about the following factors:

  • Zepp is facing tough competition this year, and the macro setup doesn't help. The company is coming off the back of major product launches in 2025 that will make it difficult to maintain double-digit growth, especially in a tougher macro environment where more consumers may choose to hold onto their watches for longer.
  • Relatively weak margin profile. The company maintains gross margins in the high 30s, which have been battered by tariffs and are ~20 points below Garmin. This severely hamstrings Zepp's ability to scale.

From a valuation standpoint, we note that, at least on a revenue basis, Zepp trades at cheaper revenue multiples than its closest pure-play peer, Garmin:

That said, profit is a different story. Zepp's razor-thin margins lead to a low denominator from an EV/EBITDA perspective, and against the bottom line, it's Garmin that looks like the relative bargain. We think that without a meaningful change to Zepp's gross margin profile, its ability to scale will be limited.

All in all, with the plethora of high-quality software businesses for sale right now amid the "SaaSpocalypse" trend, there's no need to strain ourselves to find growth-at-a-reasonable-price stocks. Zepp has lost its appeal; in my view, it's time to start trimming this position.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Zepp's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Zepp's revenue grew 43% y/y to $85.2 million. While this came in on the higher end of the company's guidance range of 38-45% y/y growth for the quarter, I'm disappointed in the revenue results, as the company had cited Q3 (which grew at 78% y/y, buoyed by the launch of the new $399 T-Rex Pro rugged smartwatch) was constrained by both typhoon conditions in China and supply constraints on the $99 Helio Strap that pushed some orders out from Q3 to Q4. I had hoped that, combined, these two factors would have pushed more revenue to Q4 and blunted the deceleration between the two quarters.

In Q4, Zepp's gross margin rose 360bps y/y to 40.4%, which the company billed as an all-time record. The company notes that the margin improvement was achieved due to the fact that the company hit strong holiday-quarter sales without heavy discounting, which is a big plus.

That said, quarterly gross margins can be influenced by product mix, especially as Zepp's product launch timing is more irregular. When we smooth this out over the course of the year, we are more concerned about the company's 38.3% full-year margin, which fell -170bps y/y to 38.3% for Amazfit-only products (which is the company's focus going forward), and -20bps against the full product portfolio in FY24, which included some Xiaomi-branded sales.

Now, it's not all bad news for Zepp. In February (Q1), the company just launched the $550 T-Rex Ultra 2, which it billed as its highest-priced product ever. If sales are successful and Zepp/Amazfit can continue migrating customers toward these pricier watches, we could see gross margins on an upward glide path.

Per CEO Wang Huang's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In our Premium portfolio, the T-Rex and Balance series continue to perform strongly. In February, we launched T-Rex Ultra 2, our newest flagship outdoor watch, built with Grade 5 titanium and designed for achieve durability, Ultra 2 extends the top end of our portfolio to around the USD 550 price level. The highest price point in our history. Products like Ultra 2 reinforce the premium positioning of the Amazfit brand while expanding the selling of our product portfolio."

The other good news is that the company has been able to offset gross margin headwinds with opex reductions. In FY25, opex fell to 47.8% of revenue, a ~13 point reduction from 60.5% in FY24, as the company achieved economies of scale on its much larger sales base on R&D and sales and marketing costs.

Still, we worry about overall profitability: despite margin improvements, in Q4 the company's EBIT was still negative at -$3.0 million; while full-year EBIT was -$24.4 million (a -40% smaller loss than FY24, but perhaps not enough appease investors in a more risk-averse market).

Key takeaways

I've been disappointed with Zepp's recent share price performance, but I'm no longer quite as confident that the company's strong revenue growth trends and market share gains in smartwatches justify an upward re-rating for this stock without the company proving that it has a path to richer gross margins. This is especially true when rival Garmin has guided to a 58.5% gross margin in FY26, or twenty points richer than Zepp (which justifies a major chunk of the revenue multiple premium against Zepp):

In my view, it's best to move to the sidelines on Zepp for now, until we get confirmation on stronger sales for higher-end products that can move the margin profile higher and push Zepp to positive EBIT.

data.title: USA Compression Partners: A High-Yielding Income Idea
data.description:
The last time I co-wrote an article was years ago when USA Compression Systems (USAC) became part of the Energy Transfer (ET) family of companies. To this day, Energy Transfer owns the general partner and so controls the daily operations of the company. This partnership has long sold itself as an income idea. With the expected recovery of natural gas prices, a company like this may well benefit from the steadily increasing volume of natural gas production expected in the future.

There are many areas of oil production that become gassier as the production ages. This introduces a countercyclical element to the equation that offsets (somewhat) the downside potential of any company that operates in this industry.

On the other hand, anything involving oil and gas is cyclical in nature. That introduces an element of risk that may not be acceptable to some income investors. To counter that cyclical nature, this company tends to market its products towards parts of the market that are not cyclical. One example of this would be natural gas power generators. That business is not only longer term in nature than is the case with upstream. But it also does not really have a cyclical nature to it.

This marketing strategy underpins the income idea for the common units. The success of the income model is very dependent upon the success of that marketing strategy.

What helps here is that this is a very large organization. That size enables some differentiation in the structure to service different ideas for the use of its products that smaller competitors cannot deal with. As a result, this is one of the few income ideas in the compression industry that has succeeded (at least so far).

Performance History

The current five-year history benefits from the general upstream recovery that took place after fiscal year 2020. Long-term readers may well remember that at one point, the United States production actually decreased 3 million BOD (very roughly) due to the COVID challenges.

That production came back online towards the end of fiscal year 2020 and later, as oil prices again became positive and later rallied toward very high prices in fiscal year 2022.

For the five-year history that is being presented throughout the industry (in various forms), the result is an unusually attractive five-year record, as shown above. That result is much more likely to return towards the long-term return here of mostly distributions with a little bit of appreciation.

Going forward, it is reasonable to expect a nominal distribution increase each year along with some sort of single-digit common unit price appreciation potential. If there is a period of inflation ahead, this may not be the vehicle to keep up with an inflationary trend.

On the other hand, this company made it through fiscal year 2020 in very reasonable shape. That is in contrast to much of the industry that suffered from a whole lot of returns of compressors as production was shut-in when oil prices briefly went negative.

Even though there is a bit of risk to the income idea here, the fact that this partnership survived fiscal year 2020 shows that the marketing idea made a material difference from much of the industry to make the income idea survive a challenge like fiscal year 2020. I followed many upstream related ideas that did not make it through that period.

Diversification

This company supports the total compressor cycle from manufacturing all the way through to the end of usage of the compressor.

The company is also diversified horizontally in that it services the major basins as shown above.

Between those two forms of diversification is a lot of safety that many in this rather fractured part of the oil & gas industry cannot demonstrate.

Debt

This also supports a bit more leverage than some other companies have in the industry. The leverage here stays in the 4.0 range for the debt ratio. The overall steadiness of the business makes that financial leverage work.

The result is that from a debt perspective, Energy Transfer manages this more like a midstream business than would be typical for a lot of compression companies (that have a materially lower debt ratio). As the debt ratings above demonstrate, the whole thing works and has worked for some time.

As a side note, this is a highly fractured industry with no one company having a commanding share of the market. Therefore, periodic acquisitions are the norm (and are likely to be the norm for some time to come). Such acquisitions can provide for some incremental share appreciation potential.

Summary

The distribution was last raised in 2015. Then again, that is a much better record than much of the industry (the compression industry) that cut the distribution back in 2020 when the going got really rough.

Going forward, it looks as though finances are finally such that there may be the ability to gradually increase that dividend (for the first time in some years).

Back when I cowrote the article (years ago), the distribution yield was well past 10%. The fact that it is now under 10% indicates some market certainty about the distribution that did not exist back then.

The thing to note is that most investors report an 8% compounded return long-term that is made up of mostly dividends. In this case, that whole return is the dividend. The difference now is that there is some reserve "just in case" another industry challenge appears.

Any stock price appreciation potential going forward is "icing on the cake". That appreciation potential should push the total return into the low teens. The above average appreciation of the stock price that happened after fiscal year 2020 is now largely over with.

However, there is expected to be some decent demand for compression systems as production becomes gassier in some basins (as unconventional production ages). The demand for compressors that comes with all the exporting projects is also a macro tailwind as well.

This is considered a strong buy idea (with some caveats) in that the compression industry is expected to benefit from increasing natural gas production and usage in the future. The caveats would be that the industry outlook needs to be monitored continuously, and the economy in general needs to be reviewed for the future outlook.

Risk

This income idea would be considered an elevated risk situation because the economic outlook is very important, and so is the natural gas pricing outlook. In the past, this partnership has made it through a lot of challenges without cutting the distribution. But there is no assurance that will not happen in the future.

Finances are much better than they were years ago when I cowrote the previous article. As long as that remains the case or the situation improves, then this idea remains in place. However, should finances deteriorate, I would exit the position before there is trouble (potentially).

data.title: The Trade Driving The S&P 500 Higher May Not Last
data.description:
Many of the mega-cap stocks have rallied significantly over the past week, driving the S&P 500 to outperform the equal-weighted S&P 500 ETF (RSP). This is an interesting development because it appears to be the same dispersion trade seen in previous quarters.

What makes the current period more notable is that the credit markets are not confirming the move witnessed in mega-cap technology stocks. Credit Default Swaps are not validating the move in some stocks to the same degree.

Dispersion May Be the Driver of the Market

This is evident in the S&P 500 dispersion index, which climbed to around 36.5 this past week, peaking at 38 on Wednesday, April 15. This indicates that stocks are expected to trade independently of one another. It is another way of saying market-implied correlations are low. For the most part, this rise in the DSPX is a seasonal pattern that has recurred, rising into earnings, consolidating around earnings, and then unwinding after earnings seasons.

The idea is that a trader or fund will go long single-stock implied volatility and short index-level implied volatility, best illustrated by comparing the S&P 500 VIX constituent index to the VIX index itself. What is clear is that index-level volatility has fallen much faster and further than constituent volatility, which creates the dispersion.

It would suggest that the market is still pricing in very high levels of implied volatility for single stocks and significantly lower for the index. Typically, once mega-cap stocks conclude their earnings announcements, the dispersion trade unwinds, pushing up implied correlations and causing the market to reverse gains.

One Way to Watch

The DSPX index minus the 3-month implied correlation index tends to do a good job of indicating when the trade is taking place and when the unwind is underway. On several occasions over the past few years, the spread has reached extremes like the one it is currently exhibiting. The last few examples of this can be seen in October 2025 and February 2026. This was also witnessed in February 2025 and August 2024.

The spread can help frame potential turning points in the S&P 500 over time. Recently, the spread has widened sharply, along with the rise in the index, suggesting the move may be becoming stretched.

Credit Not Convinced

Notably, there have been significant moves among some of the index's biggest names, which have helped drive the market-cap-weighted S&P 500 index's outperformance. But what also stands out is that while the stocks have rallied sharply, the credit markets' views have not evolved.

For example, Meta (META) has seen its stock surge to its highest price since February 3. Meanwhile, the 5-year credit default swap for Meta is trading at a wider spread than at the end of February.

Meanwhile, the same can be said of Oracle (ORCL), which has also risen significantly. Meanwhile, the 5-year CDS, while improving, appears not to have improved as significantly.

A number of reasons could drive the disconnect between stocks and credit; perhaps the CDS market is less liquid than the equity market, so prices evolve more slowly in credit. Perhaps the equity market became more bearish on the two stocks than it should have been, and they were simply oversold.

While this does not typically resolve immediately, the current setup is consistent with prior periods that led to consolidation or downside. This would suggest that the risk-reward for equities may be becoming less favorable as we approach and move through mega-cap earnings.

data.title: VTV: Vanguard's Passive $169B Value ETF Is Good, But SEIV's Active Approach May Be Better
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In typical Vanguard fashion, the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV) offers investors low-cost passive exposure to a broad basket of stocks that tend to perform reasonably well under all market conditions. My educational review of the fund demonstrated that VTV rarely delivered returns in the top or bottom quartiles compared to other large-cap value ETF peers, so in that respect, it's a pretty safe bet. However, this article explores the possibility of replacing VTV with the actively managed SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value Factor ETF (SEIV), which has an expense ratio of just 0.15% and better total returns since its inception on May 17, 2022.

As I'll demonstrate below, SEIV features a substantially discounted forward P/E ratio (11.44x vs. 17.08x), 1-4% more historical and estimated EPS growth, and quality features that are at least just as solid. Although more risky and not quite as diversified - SEIV holds 117 stocks compared to 309 for VTV - I think this tradeoff is worth it for the better factor exposure SEIV offers. Therefore, I've rated VTV a "hold" and SEIV a "buy," and I hope you enjoy this head-to-head analysis comparing the two.

VTV vs. SEIV: Important Strategy Differences Worth Noting

As I mentioned earlier, VTV is a passively managed, rules-based fund, while SEIV is actively managed and follows what I call a "single style" strategy, which is not to be confused with a "single factor" strategy. This means that SEIV won't necessarily hold value stocks that score poorly on other factors, namely growth, quality, and momentum. In this sense, it behaves more like a multi-factor ETF, but undoubtedly, the value lean is the strongest. That will become evident later when I highlight its fundamentals.

VTV, on the other hand, also applies a multi-factor screening process, but not in the same way. Since April 16, 2013, it has tracked the CRSP Large Cap Value Index, which is a float-adjusted, cap-weighted Index that draws from an Index comprised of the top 85% of the total U.S. market capitalization. The multi-factor models used separate value from growth, but importantly, the value stocks selected tend to rank poorly on the growth factors, and vice versa, based on the criteria below.

In practice, this means that VTV's ideal stocks would have low P/E and P/B ratios and high dividend yields, but also low sales and EPS growth rates. In addition, since ROA is a factor in the growth model, VTV could easily tilt away from quality. These potential growth and quality deficiencies are things that SEIV managers won't accept too much of, and that's evident when evaluating the two ETFs fundamentally. As shown below, SEIV has the advantage on 3Y EPS CAGR (6.50% vs. 5.72%), 1Y Estimated EPS Growth (14.12% vs. 10.02%), and ROA (8.29% vs. 6.69%).

Lastly, the table above highlights how much more popular VTV is compared to SEIV. Its AUM is enormous at $169B, and its 0.03% expense ratio is the cheapest among all the U.S. large-cap value ETFs I track. For its part, SEIV isn't bad either, with $1.2B in assets under management since its May 2022 launch. Furthermore, its 0.15% expense ratio is tied for second place among all actively managed U.S. large-cap value ETFs behind the WisdomTree US Value Fund (WTV). However, SEIV's 0.12% 30-day median bid/ask spread is elevated compared to VTV's 0.01%, so there are some differences in terms of liquidity to consider. Over the long run, I don't think this will matter much, but certainly if you decide to buy SEIV, I recommend using limit orders and avoiding buying at the market open, when spreads tend to be the widest.

VTV vs. SEIV: Performance Comparison

How VTV Wins By Not Losing

If you read my educational review of VTV, you might remember that it was one of only six U.S. large-cap value ETFs to never deliver fourth-quartile returns over the last ten years. The five others were:

  1. Schwab US Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV)
  2. SPDR S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV)
  3. Schwab Fundamental US Broad Market ETF (FNDB)
  4. Vanguard Mega Cap Value ETF (MGV)
  5. WisdomTree U.S. Total Dividend Fund (DTD)

I don't want to understate the importance of "winning by not losing," and VTV is an excellent example of how you don't need big wins to succeed. Remember that a 20% decline requires a 25% recovery just to break even, and over the last ten years, VTV's worst year was in 2018, when it declined by 5.44%. That's manageable for most investors, and the result has been a compounded 211% total return since May 2016 that ranks #13/51 among all U.S. large-cap value ETFs with that ten-year history. For a complete list of these results, please refer to the table below.

VTV also ranks:

  • #18/51 on volatility (annualized standard deviation)
  • #18/51 on maximum drawdowns
  • #29/51 on "Best 12 Months" return
  • #20/51 on "Worst 12 Months" return
  • #13/51 on risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe Ratio)
  • #14/51 on downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio)

These rankings reinforce VTV's "winning by not losing" profile. In short, it won't blow you away with huge one-time gains, but it also won't put your capital at too much risk, and that's proven to be a winning strategy over the long term.

SEIV: Track Record Is Short But Impressive

On the other hand, SEIV's four-year track record is likely too short to draw many meaningful conclusions. However, it's still impressive. As shown below, it's delivered a 77.38% total return since June 2022 compared to 54.56% for VTV, and even though its 13.21% max drawdown was about 3% more, it's demonstrated an ability to bounce back quickly. In fact, its "Worst 6 Months" return of -3.46% was actually 3% better than VTV's. This is a good reminder that unless one plans to frequently trade these ETFs based on current market conditions (which is not my suggestion), it's important to value the recoveries just as much as the drawdowns.

Still, it's reasonable to question if SEIV is simply benefitting from a favorable launch date. We know from the earlier statistics that it intentionally does not ignore other non-value factors, including momentum. And, given the unusual strength of the momentum factor over the last three years, SEIV's substantial outperformance over this period is perfectly explainable. However, it's difficult to say what will happen if the momentum factor falls out of favor for an extended period. We just don't have the performance data yet, so while I acknowledge SEIV shareholders are flying a little bit blind right now, I think we can still learn a lot about potential risks by evaluating its composition and fundamentals, so let's do that next.

VTV vs. SEIV: Sector Allocation Comparison

VTV and SEIV have substantially different sector compositions. I would say that with 22.25% allocated to Financials, VTV functions more like a traditional value ETF, given how cheap P/E, P/B, and P/S ratios are typical in that sector. In contrast, SEIV has 30.40% allocated to Technology, which is actually the 12th highest among the 148 U.S. large-cap value ETFs I track. It's no secret that tech stocks have outperformed the broader market since SEIV's launch, so let's add that to the list of possible reasons it's done so well over the last four years.

VTV also overweights sectors with pricing power, namely Consumer Staples (10.02% vs. 4.90%) and Health Care (15.58% vs. 12.13%), which should give it an edge in high-inflation environments. Note that inflation has been trending downward for much of SEIV's existence, but depending on the outcome of the Iran War and the direction of oil prices, that could change in 2026. According to the latest University of Michigan Survey of Consumers, "Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 3.8% in March to 4.8% this month, the largest one-month increase since April 2025." In this scenario, I can see VTV having the edge over SEIV, and YTD through April 16, 2026, it is slightly outperforming (6.37% vs. 4.28%).

VTV Fundamental Analysis: Top 10 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for VTV's top ten holdings, which comprise 21.06% of its portfolio. I've already covered VTV's growth deficiencies relative to SEIV earlier in this review, but you might be surprised to learn that VTV also has higher P/E ratios than SEIV. For example, its 21.18x trailing P/E ratio represents a 35% premium over SEIV's 15.74x, so from a growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective, it's not even close - SEIV wins in a landslide.

However, in VTV's defense, it does appear to be the less risky of the two. Its 0.84 five-year portfolio beta is meaningfully lower than SEIV's 0.98 figure, and although its ROA is lower, it does hold the edge over SEIV on EBIT margins (21.76% vs. 19.11%) and ROE (21.24% vs. 20.87%). Furthermore, SEIV appears to have lost the price momentum advantage that has carried it to such strong returns over the last few years. Today, its components' weighted average one-year price returns are excellent at 55.18%, but VTV isn't far behind at 46.32%.

SEIV Fundamental Analysis: Top 10 Holdings

Next is SEIV's top ten holdings list, which totals 26.44% of the fund, or about 5% more than VTV. Here, we see the strength of SEIV's big tech plays come out. Its modest allocation to Nvidia (NVDA) gives it a growth, quality, and momentum boost, as does its 2.53% allocation to Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL). These are smart holdings because even though their P/E ratios are high and their dividend yields are low, they still offer excellent value for their price. They're market leaders for a reason, so rather than outright avoid them like VTV does, SEIV's managers have chosen to keep them but constrain their weight. To me, this makes more sense and represents a bit more of an "evolved" approach to value investing.

Another advantage SEIV has is better earnings surprises, which I calculated at 8.50% based on last quarter's results. In addition, SEIV's Seeking Alpha EPS Revision Grades are better. If one were to convert the grades on a ten-point scale, SEIV's weighted average score would be 6.77/10 compared to 5.99/10 for VTV, so arguably, its components have earned their higher one-year price returns.

Investment Recommendation

VTV and SEIV are strong large-cap value ETFs that operate very differently from each other. On the one hand, VTV is more of a traditional value play with high exposure to the Financials sector and a solid performance track record that "wins by not losing." I think it will appeal to risk-averse investors looking for straightforward, low-cost value factor exposure, but one word of caution is that its index intentionally selects stocks with lower sales and earnings growth rates and return on assets. This strategy is not necessarily meant to benefit investors, but instead, it's designed to cleanly separate the CRSP Large Cap Index into Value and Growth baskets. That drawback leads me to a simple "hold" rating for VTV.

On the other hand, SEIV also has excellent value factor exposure, evidenced by its 15.74x and 11.44x trailing and forward P/E ratios. Unlike VTV, its active managers can ensure good exposure to other important factors, namely quality, growth, and momentum. It's less diversified and has a slightly higher 0.15% expense, but overall, I find its total factor mix to be superior. Still, SEIV is not without risk. It has a higher five-year portfolio beta (0.98 vs. 0.84), and it's not yet known how well its managers can adapt to a potential high-inflation market where momentum stocks are less dominant. I'm optimistic, and my caution isn't enough for me to back off my previous "buy" rating. Still, it's probably not best for risk-averse investors with shorter time horizons, so if that describes you, I'd steer you towards VTV.

Thank you for reading, and I hope you found the information in this article helpful. As always, please reach out with any questions on VTV, SEIV, or any other U.S. equity ETF you're eyeing, and I hope to keep the conversation going with you in the comments section below.
